data.title: Ceragon Networks: Why I See Compelling Upside From Here
data.description:
Thesis

Ceragon Networks Ltd. (CRNT) is a microcap Israeli provider of wireless backhaul solutions that help mobile operators move massive amounts of data across their networks. When I last covered the stock, I rated it a Buy on the view that its valuation didn't do a good job reflecting improving profitability and strong exposure to 5G buildouts, particularly in India.

Almost immediately after that update, shares skyrocketed. But that momentum, unfortunately, didn't last very long.

Even after looking at the most recent earnings report, I still believe CRNT is a good investment. The main reasons the company is growing are still strong, and the management team is continuing to do a good job focusing on the most important areas.

Ceragon Networks: Reasons for Optimism

North America put up $32.3 million in Q4, so it’s clearly their top region at the moment. More importantly, CSP execution seems solid, and that’s actually flowing through to results. And then there’s the backlog. Per the January update, it nearly doubled from the end of 2024. That’s a nice step up from the softer quarters earlier last year. For example, Q2 was $27 million, so this move in Q4 shows real momentum.

Meanwhile, customer engagement sounds like it's improving, and more importantly, it’s converting. There were lots of examples noted on the call, from anticipation of the Mobile World Congress next month to conversations with North American Tier 1 carriers. With respect to the latter, CEO Arazi noted that

“The level of engagement is very high. We are actually having weekly and biweekly follow-up discussion with the customer as they are looking into … tuning the solution … And in parallel, we are moving and discussing with the customer other opportunities.”

India looks solid, too. Q4 came in at $24.7 million, which puts them on roughly a $100 million annualized run rate. And with early 2026 bookings already locking that in, visibility just looks better. That’s a welcome change from the choppy demand they were dealing with in mid-2025.

With revenue accelerating and backlog expanding, the financial profile is strengthening as well. Ceragon ended with $19.4 million in net cash, compared to $10.1 million last year. This improvement came from bringing in more than $15 million in cash from operations and reducing outstanding customer payments to 107 DSO (Days Sales Outstanding). This shows better execution, including collecting overdue payments in India.

And thanks to a better "level of intimacy" in conversations, the company is winning competitive private network contracts, including a multimillion-dollar order from a utility company in the APAC (Asia-Pacific) region for full turnkey solutions. This project builds on Siklu’s 60 GHz technology, which, by the way, is used for security systems and small-cell backhaul. Basically, demand's increasing because this tech is a cost-effective solution for short-distance communication needs. The company is also seeing similar growing interest in FR2 frequency bands.

This feels repeatable to me. They are seeing growth in their order backlog in North America. Add to that steady orders coming from India, and they plan to launch four new products this year. They’re tied to what customers are actually asking for. For example, their E-band equipment matches recent industry PoC (proof-of-concept) trials in North America and Europe that focus on increasing network capacity.

More or less, Ceragon does have strong control over these efforts because it manages its own R&D and go-to-market strategy. At the same time, external factors are also helping, especially ongoing 5G rollouts in India and among North America Tier-1 telecom operators. And importantly, they’re leaning into resiliency use cases, which should help offset any slowdown in the more traditional parts of the business.

When we compare it to peers, Aviat Networks (AVNW) just reported revenue of $111.5 million. That was slightly lower than the same quarter last year, but over the first six months of the year, revenue grew 5.9%. It actually looks a lot like what we’re seeing with Ceragon in North America. That is, both are leaning into private networks and 5G upgrades. Makes sense given where the market is headed. Wireless backhaul tied to 5G transport is still expected to grow around 16% CAGR through 2028.

Also, Cambium Networks (CMBM) recently received about $45 million in orders, mainly related to customers upgrading or switching their Wi-Fi systems. However, Ceragon Networks has a strong advantage in this area because it specializes in microwave backhaul technology (the wireless links that connect network sites together). So I think this gives Ceragon an edge over Cambium in these types of network upgrade projects.

The Bear Case

Revenue's the big sore spot, down 23% YoY to $82.3M, and full-year revenue declined 14.1% to $338.7M. Q4 revenue also declined sequentially from Q3, with North America and India accounting for roughly 69% of total sales. While those were the “strongest regions in terms of revenue for the quarter.” Other regions collectively represented about 31% of Q4 revenue, highlighting the current concentration in NA and India.

Management said this was just “regular seasonality.” On top of that, some orders that were supposed to land in Q4 slipped into Q1. They also pointed out that some customers make short-term calls. Sometimes they order way more. Most importantly, they were clear: “We are not seeing anything today that changes our business view over the year.” In other words, their full-year outlook hasn’t changed. And looking ahead, they’re guiding to “reasonable recoveries in other regions” this year.

Around the world, 5G network rollouts are slowing down. On top of that, the strong Israeli shekel was hurting results. Basically, for every 1% move in the exchange rate, operating margins could drop by about 0.1%. Management's trying to reduce this risk through hedging; of course, they can’t fully control currency movements. And memory component costs are also rising sharply. In this regard, the company's trying to switch to alternative options, which is something they can manage through execution. However, the overall supply chain situation is bigger than just them.

During the Q&A, management said the issue was “not material,” meaning they don’t think it’s a big deal. However, analysts pushed back, especially about payments coming from India. DSO improved. But I still think that over three months is a long time to wait to get paid.

All of this is weighing on profitability. Margins were 34.3%, flat year over year. Not terrible. But no real progress either. More importantly, operating income dropped to $3.4 million from $12.2 million. That’s a big step down.

Opportunity with Conditions

FAST Graphs estimates the company’s fair value at $5.52 per share, based on how its adjusted earnings have historically related to its stock price.

If the company achieves ~50% growth (per FG's metrics), that could mean an estimated 149% annual return.

Meanwhile, CRNT's blended P/E ratio is 21.44x earnings, which is a bit lower than the sector’s forward P/E average of 22.96x.

Its EV/EBITDA ratio is 5.56x, also much better than the sector median of 13.53x. Cash flow's a bonus, rising 20% YoY which helps ease dilution concerns.

However, sustained negative growth, including a projected dismal -11.57% forward EBITDA, strongly hints at potential downside risk if 5G demand fails to recover. But I think that's no secret, and the worries are already baked into the price here.

Final Verdict

All things considered this quarter, and factoring in that harsh selloff since last February’s share price peak, I’m keeping my Buy rating on CRNT. Overall, the downside's cushioned by net cash, 5.6x EV/EBITDA, and a near-doubling backlog; the upside comes from operating leverage if revenue normalizes, potentially outsized if 5G and private network demand accelerates. I already sold around half of my position, taking some profits after that nice spike. This goes into the high-risk portion of my portfolio; still, for the most part, I have extremely high confidence in Israeli-run companies, so I have little issue starting to accumulate here again.

Editor's Note: This article covers one or more microcap stocks. Please be aware of the risks associated with these stocks.

data.title: Tesla's Renaissance Has Come (Rating Upgrade)
data.description:
As you can see in the rating history chart below, I have flip-flopped on my expectations for Tesla, Inc. (TSLA) stock a few times in 2025. This isn't really that much of a surprise in my view, since the company is one that is quite hard to evaluate as they transition away from being primarily a car manufacturer into robotics and autonomy.

In my latest update back in November, I criticized the stock's excessive valuation and Musk's huge compensation package and downgraded the stock to a sell. As of this writing, Tesla is down around 12% since the publication of that article. With the company having reported earnings recently and having announced a major strategic shift, I believe an update is now much needed.

In the below discussion, it is shown that Tesla's current business is in a world of pain. Revenues are declining and deliveries are deteriorating. However, the focus is no longer on their legacy business. The company's robotaxi efforts are progressing, and Optimus is set to lead the company in the robotics era. While this new direction will require quite a bit of time and CAPEX to play out, I believe that Tesla's renaissance has finally come.

With that being the case, the valuation is likely not anywhere near as expensive as implied by current multiples. While I have flip-flopped on the stock quite a few times in the past year, Tesla does deserve a Buy rating at this point.

Q4 Financial Review

We'll start off today with a review of Tesla's Q4 earnings results, even if they aren't the most important driver for the stock's performance moving forward. In the fourth quarter, the company generated total revenues of $24.9 billion, and you can see above that this represents a 3% decline YoY. This is a deterioration from the previous quarter, but there have certainly been worse revenue declines seen in the past few years. As a whole, I would say that Tesla's existing car-focused business is looking shaky as business activity hasn't found any sustained growth in recent memory. With that being the case, it does seem strategically correct for them to look for other growth drivers. Still, Tesla actually beat analyst expectations by a whopping $121.07 million, and so things could have been much worse.

In terms of Tesla's margins, there was a mix in their results. GAAP gross margin actually expanded by 386 basis points YoY to 20.1% as a signal that their cost control has improved. However, this strength didn't translate into operating margin robustness, with there being a 50 basis points contraction in the metric.

While it seems quite concerning that operating expenses soared by 39% YoY, you can see on their income statement that one of the largest drivers of this is an increase in R&D expenses and also much higher restructuring expenses. While these factors are a drag on profitability, I don't think this is truly bad news at all. The larger R&D budget shows that Tesla is back to work and trying to innovate in new areas, while the restructuring expenses seem necessary in my view, as the company's legacy car business is performing poorly in key markets. Therefore, I view the jump in operating expenses as crucial for Tesla's future and not concerning at this point.

A key profitability metric is adjusted EBITDA margin, and there was a 17 basis point drop YoY, but as I said above, profitability weakness is mainly driven by justified expenses. As for the bottom line, Tesla reported $0.50 for Q4 EPS, and while this is a 32% decline, they still beat estimates by $0.05 as another signal that things could have been worse.

Cars Are No Longer A Growth Driver

Tesla's car business has seen shaky results in recent times, and Q4 was certainly no exception. In terms of production, there was a decline for both the Model 3/Y and their other models. Things get even more concerning when you look at the deliveries, as the plunge in these stats shows that demand for their cars is plummeting. With their primary business being so weak, it is no surprise that the company is being forced to pivot.

Beyond the pure numbers, there is a lot that shows Tesla's car business is on a downward trajectory. On Feb 3rd, China announced that they're banning hidden car door handles. While cars that are already regulatory approved have until 2029 to comply, this shows that Tesla's designs are under scrutiny. Furthermore, it is reported that European regulators are also looking at stricter rules for electric car door handles.

The company is also losing its lead in key markets as a sign that Tesla's position in the global EV race is weakening. On Feb 5th, it was reported that Volkswagen (VLKAF, VWAGY) has overtaken Tesla as the top EV seller in Europe. Days later, it was reported that Xiaomi's (XIACY, XIACF) EV sales outperformed Tesla in January. This is a sign that Tesla is feeling the pressure of intense competition in one of the most important global markets for EVs. Back in the U.S., their market share has actually improved. While this seems quite encouraging, note that their sales still dropped 17% for January. This signals that there is a broad slowing of EV adoption in the U.S., and so even in their domestic market, things aren't going to plan, it seems.

As a whole, I would say that both their Q4 results and recent news items clearly show that Tesla's legacy business is on a highly unfavorable trajectory. If the company doesn't transition into other areas like robotics and autonomy, prospects would be dim to say the least.

Autonomy & Robotics Pivot

One of the areas that Tesla is making notable strides in is autonomous technologies, and that is fully demonstrated in their robotaxi ambitions. As you can see, cumulative paid miles have been increasing steadily since mid-2025, and now in Austin, Texas, unsupervised is ramping up. This is indicative of a clear push to become a leader in autonomy. Furthermore, robotaxi is expected to launch in cities in multiple states in H1 2026, and so this is quite clearly one of the most promising areas for the company moving forward.

As for their car business, Tesla is transitioning into autonomy-as-a-service. Full Self-Drive, or FSD, will no longer be a one-time add-on. Instead, they are moving to a subscription model, and this is aimed at lowering the barrier to entry for consumers wanting to try their autonomous technology. In my view, this change in business model should be supportive of the adoption of FSD over time and boost their overall autonomy efforts.

The even more exciting part of Tesla's future is of course their robotics efforts. In the earnings call, it was announced that the company will be ending production of the Model S and X. The factory space at Fremont will be converted for Optimus production and output could eventually hit 1 million units a year. Firstly, the wind-down of the Model S and X really signals the end of an era and a clear shift in focus for the company from cars to robotics. Moving forward, Tesla is set to unveil Optimus Generation 3 in Q1 as they look to offer significant upgrades after their version 2.5. Therefore, there are good signs that they are continuing to innovate as they prep for a commercial launch.

With the company's vision being very ambitious, there is little doubt that large investments will be needed to create the future that Musk envisions. In 2025, Tesla had a total of $8.527 billion of CAPEX, down 25% YoY. For 2026, they now expect CAPEX to be north of $20 billion, as Optimus, FSD, and Cybercab will all need significant budgets to reach their potential. Investors should therefore be prepared for large hits to FCF in the near future even though these investments are crucial for the long-term positioning of the company.

At this point, I would say Tesla has entered a renaissance. Their legacy business is seeing a deterioration in trajectory, and while that is undesirable, the stock price is always a forward-looking number. With the company seemingly gaining traction in their autonomy efforts and set to be one of the leaders in robotics in the U.S., I would say Tesla now has a well-laid-out vision. Of course, while execution over the next few years will be critical to their success, I believe that they have gotten the first step right. That should be encouraging for shareholders and prospective investors.

Valuation Isn't That Scary

Despite the recent contraction, Tesla's forward P/E stands at a lofty reading of 196.82. This level of multiple is likely enough to scare off a great deal of investors, but in my view, the valuation may not be as ridiculous as it seems.

Firstly, the forward P/E is based off of consensus estimates for the next four quarters. Tesla now has a solid vision, but true financial payoff from their robotaxi and robotics efforts isn't going to happen quickly. This new direction is promising but will likely take years to fully show up in their financial results. Therefore, earnings projections are really mostly reflective of their legacy car business and not indicative of how strong the long-term growth story really is.

While it is nearly impossible to put into numbers how large the opportunity in autonomy and robotics is, I will say that early targets like 1 million Optimus robots coming out of the Fremont factory show that the potential for financial growth is sizeable. As a result, the forward P/E is a bit misleading, as Tesla likely isn't anywhere near as overvalued as implied by the multiple.

Conclusion

At this point, Tesla has become a potentially rewarding stock for long-term investors. With their legacy car business falling out of investor focus, all eyes are now on their vision. Moving forward, there is definitely execution risk and notable competition, but I would say that Musk's pivot is justified and is likely Tesla's best chance at success. As for the valuation, as stated above, investors shouldn't let the elevated forward P/E deter them, as it really doesn't account for the long-term growth story. I believe recent developments show that a renaissance has finally come for the company, and as a result, I have decided to shift back to a bullish stance.

data.title: Tourmaline Oil: Why I'm Buying Alongside The CEO
data.description:
Tourmaline Oil: Strong Buy Ahead of Earnings

Tourmaline Oil (TRMLF) remains a Strong Buy ahead of the company’s Q4 2025 and full-year results (expected on March 4, 2026.)

When I first upgraded Tourmaline to Strong Buy in February 2024, natural gas was near cyclical lows, and it felt like nobody wanted to own Canadian gas producers. Since then, the thesis has become materially stronger: Production is up more than 20%, the base dividend was hiked close to 50%, LNG Canada has gone from a future catalyst to a functioning export terminal, and we received news of a possible sale of Peace River assets to Canadian Natural Resources (which should unlock $1 billion-plus in proceeds).

Yet, the stock has barely moved through all of this, trading mostly sideways since then. The gap between the stock’s fair market value and its current price is the widest it has ever been.

Investor upside from here is at least 25% at spot natural gas prices, with low risk. And it's a balance sheet that could theoretically absorb a $5 billion acquisition and yet still carry less leverage than some of its peers today.

Tourmaline remains the single best risk-adjusted way to play the structural tightening of Western Canadian natural gas, as I argue below.

What Has Changed Since My Last Article

Previously, Tourmaline was producing 500,000 boepd, which already made it among the largest North American natural gas producers.

Production has only grown since my last coverage: As of Q3 2025, production averaged 634,750 boepd, and Q4 guidance calls for 655,000 to 665,000 boepd with an exit rate pushing 700,000. That’s a sharp 27% to 40% increase in under two years.

You'll see above, however, that Tourmaline's growth upside is huge as it targets more than 800,000 boepd by 2030.

The biggest development since my last coverage is related to LNG Canada. The Shell-led terminal in Kitimat began production in mid-2025 and shipped its first cargo in June.

CEO Mike Rose has stated that LNG Canada could reach close to 2 Bcf/d of feedgas demand as early as Q1 2026. That represents more than 10% of total Canadian gas output being redirected to premium-priced Asian markets - a structural demand shift that simply did not exist two years ago.

On the shareholder returns side of things, the board raised the base quarterly dividend from $0.35 to $0.50 per share in early 2025, a 43% increase, giving shares a yield over 3%. But it has also layered on special dividends of $0.35, $0.35, and $0.25 over the first three quarters. The annualized run rate of roughly C$3.00 per share yields ~5% at the current price.

Proved plus probable reserves grew 14% to 5.5 billion boe, which gives Tourmaline a reserve life of about 21 years at current rates with 100-plus years of drilling inventory. The company said that PDP finding and development costs came in at $8.45 per boe with a 2P recycle ratio of 2.1x, among the best in the Canadian E&P space.

Tourmaline also initiated a sale of its Peace River High assets. According to multiple reports, Canadian Natural Resources is the top contender to purchase the assets; the Competition Bureau cleared the deal in late January 2026, and analysts estimate the portfolio could fetch up to $1.4 billion.

A reminder that Peace River is a fully developed, maintenance-capital asset that management views as a lower-return use of capital relative to its core NEBC Montney and Deep Basin positions. I’m fully in favor of the move.

The Bull Case for Tourmaline

Tourmaline gives investors excellent long-term exposure to rising natural gas prices as Canada's largest natural gas producer and fourth-largest conventional liquids producer; it's currently the fifth-biggest gas producer in all of North America.

It operates across the NEBC Montney (320,000 boepd, 3.3 billion boe in reserves), the Alberta Deep Basin (320,000 boepd, 1.9 billion boe), and the Peace River High, which is being divested. It's also the fourth-largest gas processing and midstream operator in the country, giving it control over infrastructure that most E&P peers lack.

Tourmaline is also a low-cost and very efficient producer: Q3 2025 operating costs of $4.80 per boe were down 7% from the first half, and management has guided to a further 7% reduction once Peace River closes (see this comment: “A successful sale of the Peace River High complex would reduce overall 2026 forecast corporate operating costs by an additional 7% per boe.”)

The free cash flow breakeven sits at just US$1.50/mcf on a maintenance budget, which virtually eliminates the risk of capital impairment even in a depressed market (see the image below for more specifics).

One thing many investors miss is the liquidity story. Production of liquids reached 147,000 bpd in Q3 2025, but Tourmaline is actually targeting growth to 200,000 bpd by 2031. Tourmaline’s updated growth plan now targets a total of 850,000 boepd by 2031, with projected free cash flow growth of 2.5x at flat commodity prices.

And then there's the pricing picture. LNG Canada Phase 1 needs roughly 1.8 Bcf/d of feedgas to run at full capacity. That's a massive new source of demand for Western Canadian gas that did not exist before. Deloitte Canada estimates it could flip the market into four to seven years of excess demand, which is exactly the kind of structural shift that lifts prices for everyone.

Long-suffering Canadian gas companies (and investors) are poised to benefit from several structural changes, including: The start-up of LNG Canada, declining production in several major U.S. basins, and growing demand from AI and data centers. - BMO Capital Markets veteran commodities analyst Randy Ollenberger.

AECO is expected to recover to C$2.93 to C$3.82/GJ in 2026, and Tourmaline has already locked in long-term supply agreements to feed the terminal directly.

Tourmaline: Key Insiders Are Buying

Always love to see insiders putting their money where their mouth is and buying up company shares.

I have tracked insider buying across the resource sector for more than a decade. There are many reasons an insider might sell. There's really only one reason they buy. When a CEO is spending millions of his own money through a period of depressed gas prices, it tells you that they believe the stock is cheap and likely going higher.

CEO Mike Rose has been the most active buyer. Between early August and mid-November 2025, Rose purchased 232,500 shares at an average price around C$58. He was joined by four other insiders, bringing total purchases over that period to 259,500 shares worth roughly C$15 million. As recently as December 2025, Rose added another 2,500 shares at C$59.99.

So, investors buying into the stock today actually would be buying at prices very close to Rose’s purchases.

Tourmaline Management is Truly Top-tier

I have a very favorable view of Rose, which helps support the bull thesis here.

It's hard not to like Rose’s track record. Rose founded Berkley Petroleum in 1993 with just $11 million. From there, he grew the company into a significant Western Canadian producer and sold the company to Anadarko in 2001 for C$1.5 billion (not a bad return on investment).

Rose didn’t retire at that point. He then founded Duvernay Oil Corp. and its Montney play and sold the company to Shell in 2008 for C$5.9 billion at a 40% premium.

Again, Rose didn’t retire. He ended up starting Tourmaline three months later, during the financial crisis. He took Tourmaline public in 2010 and has built it into the largest natural gas producer in Canada. His personal stake in Tourmaline is reportedly worth more than C$1 billion, as of writing.

Is the third time the charm for Rose? Will we see his third successful divestiture of a natural gas producer? Sale or no sale, I think the market should probably pay attention to this guy’s track record.

Earnings Preview: What to Watch

Tourmaline reports Q4 2025 and full-year results on March 4, 2026. Here's what investors absolutely need to keep an eye on.

  • LNG Canada demand ramp. Management has said LNG Canada could hit 2 Bcf/d by Q1 2026. The actual ramp pace is the single most important variable for 2026 pricing. Faster means a tighter Western Canadian gas balance and higher realized prices. Slower means another quarter or two of weak AECO.

  • Peace River sale and capital deployment. The Competition Bureau has cleared the CNRL deal, and I expect an update on timing and final terms. Investors could expect Tourmaline to land at least $1 billion in proceeds from the sale, which would reduce net debt to well under $1 billion (close to a net cash position) and certainly lead to discussions on share buybacks, higher dividends, or M&A.

  • Liquids production trajectory. Q3 came in at 147,165 bpd against a 2031 target of 200,000 bpd. I want to see Q4 track at or above Q3 and hear management reaffirm this target. Every barrel of condensate added widens the free cash flow margin at any gas price.

  • M&A commentary. Tourmaline completed the Crew Energy acquisition in 2024 and will have Peace River proceeds incoming plus a balance sheet that could hypothetically absorb a $5 billion deal given its low leverage. Any commentary on acquisition priorities, particularly in the NEBC Montney, would be welcome news.

In addition, I’d love to see the company announce a new share buyback program, given how undervalued the stock currently is.

Valuation

Tourmaline does not appear to be cheap at first glance when looking at trailing multiples.

The trailing EV/EBITDA sits in the 8x range, reflecting a year where AECO hit 30-year lows. You can see that its peers trade at lower multiples currently. The only peer more expensive on an EV/EBITDA basis is Peyto.

And, at 6.4x forward EV/CF, Tourmaline trades at a slight premium to peers. But post-Peace River sale, that drops to 6.0x, right in line with ARC and below Ovintiv, while carrying the lowest leverage in the group at 0.2x.

Therefore, I think investors buying here are paying a minimal premium for the best balance sheet and the best growth profile in Canadian gas. And it gets better: You could theoretically layer on a $5 billion acquisition (again, a hypothetical scenario, but possible) and bring leverage to roughly 1.5x, which is still at or below where ARC, Peyto, and Ovintiv sit today. No other Canadian gas producer has that kind of optionality as Tourmaline does.

Risks and What Would Make Me Downgrade

The obvious risk is gas prices. If AECO stays depressed through 2026 because producers keep growing into a market that LNG Canada has not yet tightened, cash flow will come in below the C$4.0 billion target.

Tourmaline's diversified marketing book helps a lot here (Q3 realized pricing was nearly 5x the AECO benchmark), but there's only so much that can be done if spot stays below C$2.00/GJ for an extended stretch.

There's also the question of whether LNG Canada ramps as fast as the bulls expect. Natural Gas Intelligence has noted that Canadian production growth has outpaced feedgas demand so far, and TD Cowen has cautioned that Phase 1 may not be the immediate inflection point some are hoping for.

Beyond that, if Peace River proceeds, it gets redeployed into a big acquisition, and integration risk enters the picture. And regulatory surprises in British Columbia around royalties or permitting are always a wild card with Canadian energy.

Bottom Line

Tourmaline, the largest nat gas producer in Canada, is a stock that’s easy to like here. With its investment-grade balance sheet, low-cost operations, solid management, insider buying, and strong growth upside over the next five years, it’s a superb opportunity.

Yet, the stock has been range-bound despite materially improved fundamentals, and I think the market is underpricing what LNG Canada means for Western Canadian gas companies like Tourmaline.

At roughly 6.4x forward EV/cash flow, with a PEG ratio of 0.69 and a 5% dividend yield, Tourmaline offers pretty compelling upside into a gas price recovery while providing downside protection through its fortress balance sheet and low-cost operations.

Ahead of Q4 results on March 4, I'm reiterating my Strong Buy rating. While this isn't a stock that will double or triple overnight, I feel the risk is relatively low, and the overall risk vs. reward is compelling.

This is one of the highest-conviction names in my coverage universe, and it’s currently one of my largest portfolio holdings.

Editor's Note: This article discusses one or more securities that do not trade on a major U.S. exchange. Please be aware of the risks associated with these stocks.

data.title: When Optimism Is Priced In: Investment Opportunities In The AI Era
data.description:
Transcript

Kimberley Stafford: Hello, I'm Kim Stafford, and I'm here again with PIMCO Group CIO, Dan Ivascyn, to give you an inside look at some of the recent discussions taking place within PIMCO's Investment Committee or IC. Dan, thank you for joining us!

Daniel J. Ivascyn: Thanks, Kim.

Kimberley Stafford: You kicked off the year with some global travel, spending time with our investors outside of the US. In fact, you happened to be on the ground in Asia during the recent election headlines and volatility in Japanese bonds. What stood out to you about how investors are thinking about markets? What were the range of perspectives around asset allocation and risk preferences?

Daniel J. Ivascyn: This time of year, I do tend to hit the road and meet with clients around the globe. And at these various events, we do surveys as well. So we actually have some hard data in addition to a lot of investor conversations.

There were a few themes this year. One general optimism towards economic growth, excitement about the riskier areas of the financial markets. A lot of excitement about global equities. A lot of excitement about the client's respective local market equities, but still not a tremendous amount of interest in global bonds or global fixed income.

So again, a risk-seeking mindset, but also a mindset where there is increasing anxiety or concern about some of the global themes or global uncertainty, policy uncertainty. Geopolitics was another area of focus for investors. So I think the bottom line is that investors are still confident. They are perhaps less focused on the global opportunity set than we are at the moment. But I think that creates great opportunity.

Kimberley Stafford: You've said previously that it's always important to compare the macro outlook with what's already priced into markets. When you make that comparison, where do you see pockets of value and where do you see potential signs of complacency or risks?

Daniel J. Ivascyn: We are confident about global growth. We are confident about US growth, particularly during the first half of this year. But when you look at risk markets, a lot of this is priced in. Maybe more optimism than is warranted is priced in when you look at very, very tight credit spreads, particularly tight credit spreads in the riskier areas of the credit markets, then global equities are at elevated valuations.

You combine that with the optimism I talked about earlier in terms of various client surveys and discussions, which would suggest that a lot of the good news is priced in. Our job as active allocators across markets is to look at what's embedded and implied in pricing. And a great opportunity is when you see significant optimism in one market, pessimism in the other, that invites a relative value opportunity.

Over the last several weeks alone, we've had volatility in Japan, volatility in the UK associated again with politics, which then bleeds into France. You have China at a very different part of their growth cycle, where they're dealing with disinflationary forces, while we have elevated inflation in Australia, across Europe, and in the United States.

It's a unique environment, an environment where you can do these simple relative value comparisons, and it can uncover a lot of attractive opportunities.

And I think that's why we say that in high-quality fixed income, your starting yield is a really, really good assumption in terms of the floor, in terms of expected return. But today, with steeper yield curves, more localized volatility, more policy uncertainty, you can add onto that base case yield by making thoughtful asset allocation decisions along the way, especially if you maintain portfolio liquidity, which means flexibility to react to future uncertainty.

That's very, very different than the model or the choices that investors had a decade or so ago when yields were low or negative with very little volatility, you had to lock up your money, you had to buy some of the riskier investments to generate that high yield. We're in a very different environment.

So again, we do think this is an environment where investors should acknowledge that a lot of optimism is embedded in prices. There's reasons to be optimistic, but from an asset allocation perspective, we think you could generate similar or even higher base case yields with more resiliency, more liquidity, more future flexibility than some of the popular strategies that have done well on a historical basis but probably don't do as well going forward.

Kimberley Stafford: Investors have enjoyed a long AI-led stock market boom. Lately, we've seen volatility amid AI concerns, including industry disruption, capital spending growth, and borrowing needs. From your vantage point, how is AI investment changing markets and where do we see investment opportunities?

Daniel J. Ivascyn: AI is a very important, innovative, and disruptive technology. It will, over the near term, drive growth forward. But anytime you have a disruptive technology, there’s going to be some winners and losers, and even in a strong economy, even in an environment where overall equity prices continue to go higher, they're going to be losers. So you have to be absolutely careful about this disruption. In some sense, looking back at my career in the business, I remember early stages of the global financial crisis.

People knew something was going on; didn't quite know what was going on. And again, the outcome surprised many investors. Early days of COVID. And I remember an Asia trip around the same time where I returned to the States, and it felt like something major was going on. But again, it created a lot of uncertainty. I think AI's a similar type of situation for markets.

We're in the early stages. Things are moving very, very quickly. And anytime you have this type of environment, you have to be prepared for the resulting disruption. Again, what that means to PIMCO is up in quality.

Be careful about locking up your money if you're not getting paid sufficiently to do so. Be careful about leverage. Be careful about concentrations, which gets back to this attractiveness of global diversification.

Focus on sectors that are less prone to disruption; more granular risk, like asset-backed risk, hard assets back at your investments; conservatism in terms of underwriting - all these are very, very important themes on a go forward basis. And all of these themes can be exacerbated if you have some type of negative growth shock. I think the playbook has to be different than the playbook that investors have utilized over the last decade or so.

But I think that the good news again is where we started the conversation, is that there's this very, very attractive, high-quality global opportunity set. But I do think that these asset allocation decisions will be quite consequential for absolute and relative returns going forward.

Kimberley Stafford: Great. Well, thanks very much, Dan, and thanks to all of you for joining us. We'll see you next time.

data.title: From Rides To Robots: Uber's Path Through The AV And AI Era
data.description:
Introduction

I recently wrote on Uber Technologies (UBER) back in November 2025. The article was titled Go From Rides To Riches With Uber Technologies. I discussed within the article that it had become one of the biggest battleground stocks in public markets aside from perhaps Meta Platforms (META), in part because of its treatment of gig workers, which some argue borderlines on abuse of labor laws, but also because of disruption risk due to mass adoption of autonomous vehicles.

The "pie in the sky" bull case that management and Bill Ackman are adamant to put forward is that mass adoption of AVs will actually increase the total addressable market for Uber. Uber could emerge as the central aggregator in an AV ecosystem because it already controls a large share of global ride demand and operates a highly liquid two-sided marketplace. Demand aggregation will attract more supply, and the AV fleet operators like Waymo and Tesla (TSLA) that own the capital-intensive assets would be better served to prioritize maximum utilization to increase their ROI rather than try to build the technology themselves.

Operators integrating their vehicles into the Uber ecosystem would provide immediate access to millions of riders, reduce their customer acquisition costs and maximize utilization. Uber benefits from brand recognition, sophisticated routing capabilities, advanced pricing algorithms, regulatory relationships, and a capital-light business model. In addition, its customers tend to be habitual, and may not be inclined to switch apps. These are advantages Uber has built up over the course of a decade and a half now and would likely come at tremendous cost to the AV operator to try and displace them, and go fully integrated.

Uber would continue to take commissions on rides while eliminating most of the driver-related costs. Lower ride costs could drive increased usage, and Uber’s platform could capture incremental take rates from a variety of AV suppliers, essentially increasing its profitability.

The bear case can be summed up in one word: disintermediation. If a dominant AV provider such as Waymo or Tesla did manage to establish direct-to-consumer relationships, especially in major metropolitan areas Uber operates, Uber would lose control of its most profitable markets. It may be extremely difficult for the providers to do this, but it's not impossible. Providers could subsidize rides or develop their own apps, thereby capturing higher-margin urban demand in cities like Los Angeles and San Francisco, which is where Uber currently generates a significant portion of its profits.

The risks of disintermediation have become a lot louder over the past few months as Waymos and Tesla's AVs show greater and greater promise, which is why Uber stock has sold off 27% from its highs realized in mid-2025. The company's 2024 results, however, were nothing to sneer at and showed no signs of disruption. Management also stated 5 misconceptions the market has about AVs, which I will discuss herein and discuss why I believe the stock more than prices in these risks and has asymmetric upside potential

2024 YE Financial Results

As mentioned previously, fiscal 2025 results that were released in early February were nothing to sneer at. Revenue increased to ~$52 billion, up ~18% year-over-year, driven by growth across both the Mobility and Delivery segments. Gross Bookings (the total value of transactions on the platform) increased ~19% to $193 billion.

Uber’s core usage KPI, Trips, saw strong growth in both rides and mobility. For example, in Q4 2025 alone, total trips were up about 22% year-over-year, fuelled by growth in both the number of monthly active consumers and trip frequency.

The lesser-known crown jewel of Uber's business is the Uber One Loyalty program, as increased adoption is indicative that the flywheel is spinning faster. A consumer may initially open Uber for a ride hailing, but their payment credentials, rewards balance, and other membership benefits can be applied to food delivery, grocery, and other services that Uber provides. Uber One simply strengthens this feedback loop by offering recurring benefits to its members, such as fee reductions and perks that apply across both Mobility and Delivery.

These rewards and benefits have the potential to compound but only if the user continues transacting on the platform, which creates switching costs as leaving Uber would mean forfeiting the accumulated benefits, convenience, and familiarity. Management has stated cross-platform users spend about three times as much as single-service users and retain at ~35 % better rates than those who use only one service.

At 2024 year-end, Uber One had already grown ~60% year-over-year to around 36 million members, showing acceleration in customer loyalty. Uber One membership expanded dramatically in 2025, however, as it grew to 46 million at year-end, up 53% from 2024. Of the 200 million total users, this means at least one-quarter of users are using at least one of Uber's services.

Uber not only grew revenue but also improved operating leverage. Higher adjusted EBITDA and operating margins reflect this, as they were able to increase their higher margin revenue streams such as advertising and loyalty monetization (Uber One). In early 2025, the advertising unit reached an annualized revenue run rate of over $1.5 billion, reflecting ~60 % YoY growth, one of the fastest-expanding parts of Uber’s business, but has since surpassed $2 billion on an annualized basis. Uber’s ad growth isn’t just about increasing frequency, as it benefits from high intent ad spend from users as they are essentially deciding where to spend on their trip destination and are therefore most open to suggestions from the app.

Normalized EPS grew to $2.46/share which is up from $1.35/share the year prior (a 82% YoY increase). Normalization is after one‑time accounting effects such as valuation allowance releases on deferred tax assets, investment revaluations, and other non‑operational gains. Free cash flow per share rose to its highest level in its history at $4.06/share up from $3.20/share in 2024, a 27% increase.

The free cash flow number needs to be taken with a grain of salt as Uber collects insurance premiums to cover potential accidents involving drivers. The faster Uber grows its driver base and the number of rides, the faster it collects premiums. Uber sets aside these premiums in reserves to pay out future claims, but Uber reports these premiums as an operating cash flow on the cash flow statement, which can make free cash flow look higher than what is being derived from core operations. As of F2025 this amount was $2.7 billion, so about $1.26 of FCF per share or 28% of FCF, which is not insignificant.

Addressing the AV Misconceptions

Management frames the AV debate around several misconceptions:

  • that AV growth will be zero-sum;
  • that lessons from San Francisco can be extrapolated nationally;
  • that AV operators can independently achieve high utilization at scale;
  • that scaling in only a few dense cities will capture most industry profits;
  • and that AVs will soon be capable of performing nearly all trips.

AV Growth as a Zero Sum Game

Management’s core argument is that ridesharing growth is driven by supply, not demand. Each incremental unit of supply improves customer experience through lower arrival times (ETAs) and competitive pricing, which in turn stimulates greater demand. AVs will simply create an additional supply source that will expand the total addressable market rather than cannibalize human-driver trips.

In support of this view, Uber has pointed out that in markets where AVs operate on its network, such as Austin and Atlanta, overall trip growth has accelerated rather than declined. Even in San Francisco, where Uber does not yet offer AV trips directly in their app, management has noted that Uber trip growth improved in 2025 despite the increasing presence of AVs in the broader market.

This isn't to say that this dynamic can't change if AV cost per mile falls meaningfully below human-driven alternatives. In this scenario, AVs may not simply expand supply but may actually replace it - this being if vertically integrated operators achieve sufficient scale and cost advantages.

Can AV operators achieve high utilization independently?

Uber argues that building an AV is fundamentally different from operating a global mobility marketplace. Ride demand is highly volatile by hour, day, season, and special event. Management claims that a hybrid model maximizes asset utilization because human supply can step in during peak demand while AVs operate at a steady pace during base levels of demand.

In Austin and Atlanta, Uber estimates trips per vehicle per day (TpVD) are roughly 30% higher than in other major AV markets and more than double levels observed in Los Angeles. Additionally, average AV ETAs in those hybrid markets are estimated to be ~25% lower than in other AV deployments.

The counterpoint is that these utilization advantages may be a reflection of AVs not having mature enough fleets. AV fleets are likely scaling supply ahead of demand to reduce ETAs and building trust with the consumer, which depresses vehicle utilization. As routing algorithms improve, fleet concentration increases, and as geographic zones are optimized, vertically integrated operators may close the gap as far as utilization and ETAs.

Furthermore, centralized fleet control theoretically allows AV operators to optimize dispatch and positioning in ways that decentralized human networks cannot.

Although Uber does complete as much as 10 million trips per day, and Waymo only completes 450,000 per week, which is a small fraction of the trips Uber does, Waymo's robotaxi trips grew from roughly 200,000 in early 2025, which shows rapid adoption and deployment. Waymo projects reaching 1 million weekly trips by late 2026. Not only that, but Waymo robotaxis complete more trips per day than 99% of human drivers and therefore can realize much higher utilization, so AVs really don't need to be scaled at a rapid pace to become a greater portion of the ride-share market.

Is San Francisco representative of Other Markets

They are adamant San Francisco is not the same as other markets Uber operates in. San Francisco is among the most tech-savvy markets on the planet, has a dense urban layout, an affluent customer base, shorter average trip distances, a mild climate, and a supportive regulatory framework for technological innovation. In many ways it is the perfect market to showcase AV technology and presents the best testing ground for operators like Nuro, Tesla, Waymo, and Zoox.

Uber emphasizes that utilization even within California varies meaningfully. Management points to public data suggesting AV trips per vehicle in Los Angeles are materially lower than in San Francisco, despite lower pricing in LA. Management argues that this utilization gap could pressure the economics of AVs as companies expand beyond the most ideal showcase markets.

That isn't to say that there cannot be a domino effect and that once one city like San Francisco greenlights AV technology, others won't follow. Especially in cities like Phoenix, Austin, and Miami that are known for high congestion and expensive parking but already have meaningful rideshare penetration and regulatory openness. They may fast-track the regulatory process to reduce the cars and accidents on the road if it shows proof of concept in San Fransisco. Over time, cost improvements could make even lower-income or weather-challenged markets economically viable as well.

Will AVs only need a few large cities?

Management shows unwillingness to acknowledge the idea that AVs will only need to scale in large metropolitan areas to capture the overwhelming majority of the TAM. Uber notes that its top 20 U.S. cities represent roughly 30% of Gross Bookings and 25% of profits. The broader “long tail” of suburbs, smaller cities, and rural markets contributes a substantial and growing portion of earnings.

Even within top cities like New York City, Boston, and Chicago, regulatory pathways for AV commercialization remain complex and slow-moving. As a result, human-driven supply is likely to dominate much of the geographic footprint for years.

However, dense urban cores tend to exhibit the strongest network effects, cross-sell monetization (e.g., advertising and membership), and AV economic viability. If AV cost structures end up being materially better than human-driven, long-term profit potential could shift back toward the major metropolitan areas rather than the "long tail" markets.

Will AVs soon be able to perform all trips?

Management argues that AVs remain operationally limited, citing non-service in lower-income neneighborhoodsweather-related shutdowns or delays, and infrastructure ououtages,hich shows the limitations of the AV network. Hybrid networks have been shown to provide greater resilience to these events, so human drivers can fill the demand AVs cannot.

The counterpoint is that automation may ultimately deliver greater rereliability,articularly for late nights, low-supply zones, or markets where driver elasticity is limited. Not only ththat,ut if AVs achieve superior safety, consistency, and cost performance, consumer preference may shift toward strictly using AVs. In that case, owning the full AV stack from fleet management, charging, insurance, and remote operations could prove strategically more valuable than coordinating third-party supply with Uber or Lyft (LYFT).

What This All Means For Uber

Management’s thesis rests on three pillars, which include ridesharing being supply-driven, so AVs expand rather than cannibalize demand; hybrid networks maximizing utilization and reliability as a result of volatility in demand; and geographic and regulatory fragmentation slowing down full AV displacement.

I believe in the near term, these arguments are compelling. Hybrid deployment greatly improves asset efficiency and operational resilience. Until AV fleets mature, the ability to utilize human supply during peak demand and maintain consistent ETAs provides a very tangible marketplace advantage.

Once AV fleets mature, the long-term outlook for ridesharing is uncertain. If AV cost per mile declines materially, fleet concentration increases, and vertically integrated operators solve peak-demand positioning through software rather than labor elasticity, today’s marketplace could look very didifferent, and the advantages derived by the consumer from the hybrid model may all but diminish. Control over fleet management, charging infrastructure, insurance, remote operations, and financing may prove to be core value-enhancing levers rather than value-add services. In this scenario, vertically integrated operators could capture more of the long-term margin pool than aggregators such as Uber.

Compounding this is the emergence of agentic AI. In a future where consumers increasingly rely on AI to compare ride options, sophisticated LLMs could access multiple platforms, including Uber, Lyft, Waymo, and other AV providers, simultaneously and simply select the cheapest or fastest option. This scenario would reduce the importance of Uber’s app as the primary interface consumers interact with and undermine its aim to be the aggregator of demand.

Ultimately, the AV debate is less about whether autonomous vehicles will scale, as they likely will; it is more about where the economics are realized. The outcome hinges on three variables: cost per mile convergence with the human-operated model, speed of regulatory diffusion, and whether Uber’s network advantages remain defensible in a world that becomes increasingly more reliant on autonomous fleets and autonomous software.

Valuation

At approximately $68 per share, Uber carries a market capitalization of roughly $145 billion. On headline figures, the stock trades at around ~15x earnings or 25X on an EV/EBIT basis. This places Uber in line with the premium tier of marketplace and technology platforms like Visa (V), Mastercard (MA), Netflix (NFLX). This implies the market still expects sustained double-digit growth and continued margin expansion. I prefer to use earnings or EBIT than free cash flow to value Uber as those metrics are not inflated by insurance reserves, in fact insurance accruals are an expense so actually reduces earnings.

Although the valuation may not seem "screaming cheap" today, 2025 results confirm that Uber is well on track to meet management guidance set forth for 2030. When we assume a constructive but not heroic scenario where Uber grows revenue through at a mid-double digit pace (~18% annually), supported by mid double digit booking growth in mobility and delivery, advertising expansion, and incremental take-rate improvement. If operating margins are further supported by higher contribution from ads, greater operating leverage, and less intense promotional activity, free cash flow could plausibly grow at ~20% annually over the next five years.

In this event, operating and normalized free cash flow could reach $14 billion and $18 billion, respectively. If the company is still compounding at roughly the same rate by 2030 and assuming the exit multiple remains unchanged at 25x EBIT, the implied equity value would be $244 billion. From current levels, that represents approximately ~70% upside or a ~20% CAGR over the next 5 years. For for detailed explanations of my assumptions, see my previous article.

This is before you include Uber’s equity stake in companies like DiDi and Grab, which they have valued at $9.2 billion, and its freight business. Although the latter has been unprofitable, it received an implied valuation of $3.3 billion when it was considering doing an IPO in 2023.

However, this somewhat "pie in the sky" scenario depends on several variables not just coming to fruition but coming to fruition simultaneously. These variables include, sustained bookings growth, margin expansion through advertising and cross-selling, integration of AV deployment, and minimal disintermediation from agentic AI. Each of these risks introduces uncertainty in our risk-adjusted valuation framework bringing more nuance than management guidance would suggest.

Conclusion

Uber remains a lucrative near-term play in ride-hailing services, supported by strong consumer engagement and high-margin revenue channels like Uber One and advertising. Long-term upside, depends on the evolution of autonomous fleets, regulatory assessment on their deployment, and potential disintermediation from agentic AI. When AVs achieve lower costs through scale, improved reliability, and increased adoption, having control over fleet management and the full AV stack could shift this value toward vertically integrated operators which creates terminal value risk for Uber.

Ultimately, Uber’s success hinges on its ability to maintain its role as a demand aggregator while navigating a future increasingly defined by AV technology and AI-driven consumer choices. I do believe the AV/AI risks are unlikely to be a factor in the near-term, which theoretically should not have a major impact on its current valuation when looking at this through a DCF framework. It is not a foregone concl either that Uber cannot be a demand aggregator in the long-term as AVs reach scale, which therefore would mean greater upside. For these reasons I believe Uber shows asymmetric upside potential and rate this a Strong Buy.

data.title: Pitney Bowes Moves From Hold To Buy On Stronger Fundamentals (Rating Upgrade)
data.description:
Q4 numbers for Pitney Bowes Inc. (PBI) finally broke the stock’s slide. The stock jumped about 9% after the open following yesterday's results, and that was enough for me to rethink my Hold rating. I’d stuck with Hold because sales kept dropping, the old core business kept shrinking, and there was still over $2 billion in debt on the balance sheet. That call made sense at the time; the company hadn’t found a way to stop losing sales for good, and the stock just kept drifting down, from $11.16 to $9.48 last quarter. Sales were flat, the debt was heavy, and most people figured the buybacks and dividends just weren’t enough to draw in new long-term investors.

This quarter doesn’t mean the big-picture decline in mail volume is over. Instead, what stands out is that management finally got serious about cutting costs and being picky about where the money goes. That shift paid off. Now the company’s actually generating solid cash, and the debt is coming down, even without any sales growth.

Looking at the basics: Q4 adjusted diluted EPS hit $0.45, which beat estimates by $0.07. That wasn’t because sales picked up; in fact, Q4 sales landed at $477.6 million, just under $5 million short of what analysts thought and down 7% from a year ago. The EPS beat came from running a tighter ship, not from new business coming in.

But cash flow told a different story. Cash from operations for the year closed at $383 million, up 39% compared to last year, while free cash flow was $358 million, up 24%. Earlier quarters had been stuck in place, so this was a clear turnaround, mainly thanks to better margins and smarter handling of working capital. Adjusted EBIT for the year reached $461 million, a 20% jump from $385 million last year, and total Segment EBITDA was $660 million, up from $632 million. Net debt to adjusted EBITDA is now just under 3 times, which is a big step forward for the balance sheet. The risk on the stock is lower now, and the company’s in better shape for whatever changes come next.

SendTech’s Q4 EBIT rose 24%. They cut costs and tweaked their lineup. Presort EBIT fell 20%. Management had tried pushing prices higher before, but that just made important customers leave. Now they’re changing course. Management acknowledged that prior pricing decisions hurt volumes and indicated that beginning in 2026 they will prioritize regaining profitable volume, even if it pressures near-term margins. Presort’s new pitch to customers should help get volume back up before too long, but in the meantime, EBIT will probably keep getting squeezed.

Wall Street expects revenue to stay about flat into 2026 and 2027. The consensus has sales at roughly $1.85 billion in each of those years and EPS ticking up from $1.43 to $1.6. Not a big leap, but at least earnings are set to grow a bit, and margins are much better. The stock trades at about 7 times forward earnings. The market clearly doubts how long this can last and worries about whether they’ll have to keep reinvesting. If they can just keep sales steady and keep free cash flow around $300 million, that alone could justify the stock trading at a higher multiple, since the business brings in real cash. Management’s giving money back, too; they approved major buybacks through 2025 and increased the dividend from $0.05 to $0.09 per share during 2025, roughly an 80% cumulative increase. That’s not something you usually see from a company whose sales aren’t growing.

Risks are obvious here. Regular mail keeps shrinking, so there’s just not much room for this business to grow. Cutting costs will only get them so far, especially if the customer base keeps shrinking. They’re still at risk if the plan to stabilize mail volumes in Presort stalls out, or if SendTech loses more clients than they can keep, or if they just can’t squeeze any more savings out of the business, which seems to be happening already, since they’ve gotten less out of restructuring this year than before. The strategic review could matter a lot, but it’s a coin flip: if they can pull off something like a Presort joint venture or a sale, the stock could go up. If they mess it up or make a deal that’s confusing or dilutes value, it could go the other way. Debt isn’t as scary as it was, but it’s still a lot, and with all those convertible notes, there could be more dilution if the stock stops going up. If things get worse, like if revenue drops faster or the economy turns and free cash flow falls under $250 million, the valuation could drop to 5x earnings or less, and the stock might sink to $8 as people worry about risk and liquidity.

On the other hand, if Presort starts landing new customers, SendTech stops losing accounts, and free cash flow gets back over $350 million while debt keeps coming down toward 2x, then the stock could get an 8-9x earnings multiple. That would put the share price around $13-$14, especially if investors start believing management can keep returning capital.

After a bunch of revenue and profit misses in 2025, sentiment hit bottom, but now it’s turning. The stock’s bounced after Q4, and people seem to be noticing that management’s actually delivering better results than expected, but no one’s really pricing in a full turnaround yet. So, the risk for new investors isn’t huge, but the upside if things stabilize looks underappreciated. Plus, you’re still getting a good yield.

I’m rating Pitney Bowes a Buy. It’s not risk-free; I know there’s no growth in the industry, and management still has a lot to prove, but they’re generating reliable free cash flow, paying down debt faster, and focusing on returning capital to shareholders. That’s more important now than worrying about the stock staying cheap forever. I wouldn’t call it a Hold unless free cash flow drops below $300 million, or if they go back to cutting prices in Presort just to keep volume, or if net debt rises back above 3.5x adjusted EBITDA. None of that’s happening right now. If cash flow weakens, or if management can’t win back customers in the next few quarters, I’ll rethink. But for now, with strict cost control and decent results even without sales growth, I see more room for the stock to go up.

data.title: Macro Insights: Death Of A Software Salesman, The Great Rotation, And Dot-Com 2.0
data.description:
Introduction

Welcome to the SA’s The Macro Brief! This official SA Profile will highlight our analysts' latest economic and market analysis to help investors gauge the ever-volatile financial landscape through various recurring series.

The economy is the foundation of financial markets, influencing everything from corporate earnings and consumer spending to central bank policy and inflation. Understanding economic trends, policy decisions, and sector activity can be critical when assessing market opportunities and making informed investment decisions.

Check out these must-reads from February’s first half…

Trending Themes

Fear Is Growing

Buyer Beware: The Market's AI Bubble Risk Just Got Even Bigger | Samuel Smith | 2/7/26

“A massive spending surge is quietly reshaping the market’s risk profile. What looks like innovation may be creating a dangerous new dependency. One overlooked corner of the market could benefit enormously.”

Sell The "Dead Cat" Bounce, The Bear Market Risks Are Rising | Damir Tokic | 2/10/26

“Hedge funds are making massive short bets against tech, and the recent bounce was likely due to short covering. Thus, the selloff is likely to continue as the AI bubble continues to burst. In addition, the cyclical rotation trade is likely to falter, given the recent negative economic data.”

3 Warning Signs The Stock Market Is Overdue For A Sharp Correction | High Yield Investor | 2/11/26

“While the S&P 500 (SPY) and NASDAQ (QQQ) have been on tremendous bull runs since 2023, there are three historically reliable warning signs emerging simultaneously that indicate that the market could be due for a significant correction in the near future.”

AI Capex Boom Vs. The Dot-Com Bubble: The Striking Similarities And Three Big Concerns | Hawkinvest | 2/11/26

“Many major market indexes just hit new record highs, and earnings were strong this quarter. But, underneath all of this bullishness, there are some major concerns. This is especially true when you look at the massive capex spending plans the hyperscalers updated us with when they announced their earnings reports.”

The 'Smart Money' Isn't Buying This Market | Bret Jensen | 2/14/26

“Insiders and retail investors are currently providing divergent signals about the market's direction, which should signal caution on equities. Corporate insiders and directors are selling stocks, and Warren Buffett built up a record cash hoard before exiting leadership at Berkshire Hathaway after 60 years at the helm.”

An $80 Billion Liquidity Storm May Be About To Hit Stocks This Week | Mott Capital Management | 2/15/26

“Persistent Treasury settlements are draining liquidity, pressuring the stock market with more frequent and deeper declines on settlement days. Excess liquidity from the Fed's Reverse Repo facility is exhausted, forcing new Treasury funding to come from risk asset sales. Market weakness is likely to persist until at least April, when Treasury issuance may slow post-tax season, potentially easing pressure temporarily.”

‘SaaS-pocalypse’

The Magnificent Seven Maintains Its Winning Ways Even As Software Shows Cracks | JR Research | 2/2/26

“This couldn't have been a more bifurcated times for the tech sector leaders (XLK). As the market rotation themes got underway, tech has seen an underlying disconnect in the AI value chain, hitting the software providers hard.”

I'm Buying The Software Meltdown | Jack Bowman | 2/4/26

“Software stocks have been sharply oversold on AI disruption fears, creating compelling value opportunities for patient investors. The narrative that AI will render established software firms obsolete is overstated; domain expertise and human capital remain critical. There is no substitute for expertise, despite AI efficiency gains.”

AI Bubble, Tech Funeral? Who Will Fail And Who Will Double Down? | Agar Capital | 2/12/26

“AI-driven disruption is triggering a sharp selloff in data, consulting, and SaaS companies, exposing structural vulnerabilities in their high-fee, recurring revenue models…The market now sharply distinguishes between firms monetizing AI and those burning cash without clear profitability, pressuring SaaS and service-based stocks’ multiples.”

Software Is Finally Cracking - And The Great Rotation Is Picking Up Speed | Leo Nelissen | 2/15/26

“AI disruption is accelerating market rotation, weakening the competitive edge of many software companies, and favoring value-oriented sectors like industrials, energy, and midstream. I focus on companies with durable business models, wide moats, and resistance to AI disruption.”

Conclusion

We hope our analysts' insights serve as a valuable financial compass for investors seeking to make informed investment decisions.

Thanks for reading. Tell us what you think by commenting below! If you'd like to see more, follow this account.

Please let us know if you have any suggestions you’d like to see added to this series and any ideas for new additional recurring series. We're always open to your valuable feedback.

To stay current on the latest economic and market events coming up, subscribe to Wall Street Breakfast, Seeking Alpha's flagship daily business news summary.
